AU-01HIGH
The homepage makes shoppers do the segmentation work themselves
1Add a real homepage promise and a labeled hero action
Fixes 3 findings across 2 clusters (visual-cta, audience)
Add a visible H1 plus one supporting sentence above the fitment tool, and keep the desktop button labeled `Find parts` at every state.
Underlying findings
visual-cta/F-01 — Hero asks for four choices before stating the offer
visual-cta/F-02 — Desktop primary action becomes icon-only at the moment of intent
audience/F-01 — The homepage makes shoppers do the segmentation work themselves
2Bring trust, shipping, and checkout reassurance into the decision path
Fixes 4 findings across 3 clusters (trust-credibility, pricing, checkout-flows)
Add a compact trust row under the hero and repeat key value context near product cards: review count, shipping threshold or lead-time, returns, and express checkout reassurance.
Underlying findings
trust-credibility/F-01 — Trust proof is missing beside the fitment decision
trust-credibility/F-02 — Social proof appears inconsistently across featured products
pricing/F-01 — Price is visible but value context is not
checkout-flows/F-01 — Checkout convenience cues are buried in the footer
3Make browsing faster once the shopper lands on homepage merch
Fixes 3 findings across 3 clusters (performance-ux, product-media, category-navigation)
Pull a tighter merch module higher on the page and make category/product cards carry clearer fitment and comparison information before any quick-view interaction.
Underlying findings
performance-ux/F-01 — Homepage browsing starts after multiple stacked decision layers
product-media/F-01 — Featured cards lean on quick view more than product evidence
category-navigation/F-01 — Category shortcuts do not carry the shopper's vehicle context
4Repair homepage relevance and retention signals
Fixes 2 findings across 2 clusters (content-seo, post-purchase)
Rewrite the homepage metadata around the real vehicle/value proposition, then turn the lower-page retention block into a support-and-lifecycle message instead of newsletter-only capture.
Underlying findings
content-seo/F-01 — Homepage sends weak relevance signals to search and AI systems
post-purchase/F-01 — Retention is delegated to a newsletter block instead of a support promise
No ethics findings.

Ethics findings are BLOCK (hard violations) or ADJACENT (one step from a violation). A clean slate here means nothing in the audit triggered the ethics gate.

VC-01HIGHHeadlines & Buttons
Hero asks for four choices before stating the offer

Element

hero-fitment

Observation

The above-fold desktop hero is dominated by a four-step fitment flow on top of a lifestyle image, but there is no visible homepage headline, subhead, or explicit promise about what AWDMods specializes in before shoppers are asked to choose a vehicle.

Recommendation

Add a plain-language H1 and one support line above the selector row so new visitors understand the store's focus before they invest effort in the fitment flow.

Why this matters

Visitors need a five-second answer to what the store sells and why it is relevant before they will happily complete a multi-step filter. â†³ Strong ecommerce heroes explain the offer immediately and pair the main action with essential above-fold context. [Gold]

Source

Hero Section Psychology
Open primary source ↗
VC-02HIGHHeadlines & Buttons
Desktop primary action becomes icon-only at the moment of intent

Element

hero-cta

Observation

After configuring Ford > Focus > 2018 > RS, the primary button is enabled but still renders as a 59x59 icon-led control on desktop even though the DOM text remains `Find parts`, which makes the next step look secondary and easy to miss.

Recommendation

Keep the full `Find parts` label visible on desktop and give the button enough width and contrast to read as the dominant next action.

Why this matters

High-intent shoppers hesitate when the main CTA looks like a utility icon instead of a clear continuation of the flow. â†³ Trust and CTA performance improve when the next step is explicit, prominent, and visually unambiguous. [Gold]

Source

Hero Section Psychology, Trust And Credibility
Open primary source ↗
TC-01HIGHTrust & Credibility
Trust proof is missing beside the fitment decision

Element

hero-fitment

Observation

The homepage asks for vehicle details immediately, but there are no review counts, return cues, payment reassurance, or service promises adjacent to the hero flow; the only broad trust signals visible in code are a slim free-shipping strip and footer payment icons far below.

Recommendation

Add a compact trust row directly under the hero with review proof, shipping promise, and a clear return or support reassurance.

Why this matters

Unknown and enthusiast-specialist brands need reassurance closest to the point where shoppers decide whether to continue. â†³ Trust signals work best when they sit beside the CTA instead of being buried elsewhere on the page. [Gold]

Source

Trust And Credibility, Hero Section Psychology
Open primary source ↗
TC-02MEDIUMTrust & Credibility
Social proof appears inconsistently across featured products

Element

product-rating

Observation

Some featured cards display rating and review-count modules while adjacent products present no comparable proof, which creates uneven credibility inside the same merch surface.

Recommendation

Standardize review presentation across featured cards or suppress empty review states until proof coverage is consistent.

Why this matters

Mixed proof states make the collection feel less dependable because shoppers cannot tell whether missing reviews mean low demand or missing instrumentation. â†³ Even a small review base can materially improve purchase confidence, so inconsistent display leaves money on the table. [Gold]

Source

Trust And Credibility, Product Cards
Open primary source ↗
PR-01MEDIUMPrice & Offers
Price is visible but value context is not

Element

made-to-order-badge

Observation

Product cards show prices and some `Made to Order` badges, but there is no nearby explanation of shipping threshold, lead-time implications, or return expectations, so shoppers see cost without the supporting value frame.

Recommendation

Add compact price-adjacent context such as free-shipping threshold, build-time guidance for made-to-order items, and a short returns cue near card pricing.

Why this matters

Price judgments are easier to accept when shoppers can instantly evaluate shipping tradeoffs, wait time, and downside protection. â†³ Shipping and return framing materially change conversion because shoppers use them to interpret the real cost of saying yes. [Gold]

Source

Pricing Psychology, Trust And Credibility
Open primary source ↗
CF-01MEDIUMCheckout Experience
Checkout convenience cues are buried in the footer

Element

payment-icons

Observation

Apple Pay, Shop Pay, and other payment icons exist in the footer DOM, but the homepage never surfaces comparable convenience messaging near the hero or featured collection, so painless checkout is hidden until the very bottom of the page.

Recommendation

Surface express-checkout and payment reassurance higher in the funnel, ideally near the hero and the first merch module.

Why this matters

Reassuring shoppers that checkout will be fast and familiar reduces uncertainty before they commit to browsing deeper. â†³ Early payment reassurance can improve conversion, but only when shoppers see it before they start second-guessing the path. [Silver]

Source

Trust And Credibility
Open primary source ↗
PU-01MEDIUMPerformance & UX
Homepage browsing starts after multiple stacked decision layers

Element

featured-heading

Observation

Desktop visitors encounter announcement bar, main nav, large hero image, fitment row, and a five-card category band before the featured collection begins, which delays the first real product comparison surface.

Recommendation

Compress the hero stack or pull a tighter featured product module higher so shoppers can browse sooner without clearing multiple layers first.

Why this matters

The longer it takes to reach browsable merch, the more likely casual and first-time visitors are to bounce before they see enough inventory depth to continue. â†³ Pages convert better when above-fold priority is given to the elements that help visitors act fastest, not just to decorative space. [Gold]

Source

Hero Section Psychology, Page Performance Psychology
Open primary source ↗
PM-01MEDIUMProduct Photos & Video
Featured cards lean on quick view more than product evidence

Element

quick-view-button

Observation

The featured cards use a small quick-view affordance and single-thumbnail presentation, but they do not expose richer fitment proof, alternate context, or stronger evidence on the card itself before asking shoppers to interact.

Recommendation

Use the card surface for stronger evidence first, such as fitment-compatible messaging, richer secondary imagery, or clearer spec/value cues before quick view.

Why this matters

Shoppers compare faster when the collection card itself does more explanatory work and does not require an extra exploratory click to feel credible. â†³ Better hierarchy and richer hover or secondary context improve list scanning because users can evaluate items without breaking flow. [Silver]

Source

Image Quantity Types, Product Cards
Open primary source ↗
CN-01MEDIUMBrowse & Search
Category shortcuts do not carry the shopper's vehicle context

Element

category-card

Observation

The homepage offers broad category cards like `Performance` and `Electronics`, but the cards do not restate or preserve the vehicle-selection context that the hero just requested, leaving shoppers to bridge the relevance gap themselves.

Recommendation

Add a persistent `Shop your vehicle by category` path or inject the selected vehicle into category destinations and labels once fitment is known.

Why this matters

Navigation feels easier when the site carries the shopper's context forward instead of forcing them to keep translating broad categories into their own use case. â†³ Visitors increasingly expect relevant paths to reflect their context, and frustration grows when that context is not used. [Silver]

Source

Collection Page Architecture, Personalization Psychology
Open primary source ↗
CS-01HIGHSearch Findability
Homepage sends weak relevance signals to search and AI systems

Element

meta name="description"

Observation

Raw HTML uses the generic title `AWDMods - AWDMods`, contains no meta description, and has no H1, so the homepage lacks a crawl-visible statement of category focus or value proposition.

Recommendation

Rewrite the title and meta description around AWD performance parts and install a single H1 that matches the visible homepage promise.

Why this matters

Weak metadata invites title rewrites, lowers click-through leverage, and makes the homepage less legible to both search engines and AI shopping surfaces. â†³ Search systems rewrite vague titles frequently, so clear metadata and structure are essential if you want the homepage to carry the right story. [Gold]

Source

Title Formulas SERP Psychology
Open primary source ↗
PP-01MEDIUMAfter Purchase
Retention is delegated to a newsletter block instead of a support promise

Element

newsletter-input

Observation

The lower page gives significant space to a newsletter signup, but there is no equally visible post-purchase confidence message about delivery support, review loops, referral momentum, or ownership help; `Track My Order` is only a footer link.

Recommendation

Reframe the lower-page retention block around post-purchase confidence and ownership lifecycle value, then keep email capture as a secondary action.

Why this matters

Shoppers are more willing to buy when the brand signals what happens after checkout, not just how it will collect their email. â†³ Post-purchase confidence reduces anxiety and improves repeat behavior, so bottom-of-page retention should reinforce support as well as capture. [Silver]

Source

Post Purchase Psychology, Buyers Remorse
Open primary source ↗
AU-01HIGHAudience Fit
The homepage makes shoppers do the segmentation work themselves

Element

hero-fitment

Observation

The page clearly serves AWD performance owners, but it never says which vehicles, build types, or shopper identities are best served before asking for four fitment choices, so audience recognition exists in the filter logic rather than in the message.

Recommendation

Add a short audience line that names the core vehicle communities and signals whether the store is built for daily drivers, tuners, or high-intent enthusiasts.

Why this matters

Segment-recognition copy lowers interpretation work and helps qualified visitors feel instantly understood instead of merely processed. â†³ Shoppers respond better when the experience clearly signals that the store understands their context and priorities. [Silver]

Source

Hero Section Psychology, Personalization Psychology
Open primary source ↗
